reviews.review_text:
This game is microtransaction heavy. If you were to pay full price for everything this game has to offer, then it would cost you well over $100. It was not worth that 13 years ago and it isn't worth that much today.

[u]BASE GAME[/u]

The base game is very small.

The base campaign is pretty fun and not overly difficult and has a compelling story.
You can only play as two factions in multiplayer. The soviets and the Wehrmacht. The game loves to bring up the fact that you can pay another $15 dollars for the Americans and OKW. Also it's another $12 for the British.

The theatre of war scenarios are pretty good although there are few terribly balanced ones. The content for the theatres of war is heavily front loaded.

[u]THE ARDENNES ASSAULT[/u]

The US army and OKW are sold separately from the campaign.
This campaign is substantially harder than the base game. You cannot afford to throw away divisions.

[u]THEATRES OF WAR[/u]

Their content is heavily front loaded in the southern fronts with the other containing 1/2 to 1/3 of the missions of the first.
Way better than 3 in both gameplay and balance approach

This is the multiplayer World War 2 RTS game where I spend the most hours on a video game ever on steam which I dedicated the most hours too. I had a lot of fun on this title from way back every army including the DLC felt right as well as with custom mods and experiences. I remember I made a lot of friends on this title and also was in a clan too and we did battles and multiplayer matches. I highly recommend Company Of Heroes 2 for its single player and multiplayer experience where I spend upon hours. I can say I am a veteran on this game and its one of my favorites and beloved titles here.

The game looks great for its age, and the sound effects are very well made. The controls were a bit weird at first and the game doesnt really tell you about many hotkeys, but you get used to it. I finished the soviet campaign and the missions are very fun, but the storyline is awful due to how cartoonishly evil the soviets are portrayed, playing almost every stereotype until the very ending of the story making it look like cold war era propaganda but is otherwise very enjoyable. I haven´t tried the multiplayer aspect but the matches against bots are very fun even when the difficulty just buffs them instead of getting any smarter.

Overall the game is very much worth it if you're interested in the eastern front of ww2, and for the 2 bucks something i got it for is a steal. I have played it non stop since i bought it.
Dont buy anything for full price cuz then the game is a ripoff
Terrible game, to addicting. In all serious this is a fantastic game with many accuracies to WW2 and good for the price.
mass Soviet infantryman, charge, lose all troops, repeat. would 100% percent recommend to any grade a autist.

Hi all,

this is single handedly my favorite RTS game to date. Even though CoH3 is out now and I could have bought it as a "newer model" I feel that as a new CoH2 player this game has much more than I could ever need in WW2 RTS gaming. I have no complaints. Aside from small things that may help single player skirmishing.

- Friendly AI OFTEN destroys friendly defenses with vehicles.
- Allowing key binds to be changed
- Allowing the AI to do more with defenses and abilities

(Although I am fairly new so that last could just be a matter of what difficulty I set the bots on)

Aside from those few issues I have had a blast playing this game alone and with friends. The game reminds me of playing a similar game on console when I was a child and I could not remember the name of the game for the life of me. 100% worth it on sale at $12 for all the DLC. Otherwise at full price it's still worth it if you love WW2 and RTS games. On top of that, you can always put it on your wish list and wait for a sale. They go on sale often.

Thanks,
Doc Brown#6832

Company of Heroes 2 — A Masterpiece That Deserved Better
I've played long enough to try and understand every faction, every commander, every chokepoint on every map. I love Company of Heroes 2. And that's exactly why writing this is so frustrating.
The game itself
CoH 2 is one of the finest RTS games ever made. The tactical depth is genuine — cover mechanics, resource management, a front line that actually feels alive. Soviet waves of cheap but durable units against the flexible and versatile Wehrmacht, the aggressive OKW rush with its elite infantry against the British turtle that comes online in the late game — and the Americans with their doctrine of map control and rapid territory capture. These aren't just balance decisions, they're five completely different philosophies of warfare, each demanding its own way of thinking. Winter maps with frostbite mechanics, units that feel like actual losses, firefights where positioning matters more than numbers. At its peak, the multiplayer was competitive, layered, and genuinely addictive.
The problem
Relic abandoned it. Not overnight — gradually, which is somehow worse.
Commander balance was never finished. Some are permanently overpowered, others so weak nobody touches them. Crashes, unit pathfinding issues, and various bugs have remained unchanged for over a decade. The community has documented all of this in exhaustive detail — forum posts with specific numbers, specific units, specific fixes, YouTube channels with hours of balance discussions and proposed patches. None of it was ever acted on.
Then came Company of Heroes 3. Worse sound, worse atmosphere, worse reception. Players came back to CoH 2 within days of launch. Relic noticed, said nothing, and moved on anyway.
Then the layoffs. 121 people in 2023, another 41 in 2024. The people who knew this game's codebase from the inside are mostly gone now. What's left is institutional amnesia.
What makes it sting
Fixing the core issues was never going to be expensive. Commander balance is spreadsheet work, not engine rewrites. One informed designer with community feedback and a couple of months could have meaningfully improved this game's health. That time was never allocated — because patching an old game doesn't sell as a new product on Steam.
Instead we're getting a Definitive Edition of the first game. Fair enough, I suppose. But CoH 2 had the active playerbase. CoH 2 had the competitive scene. CoH 2 was the one worth saving.
Bottom line
I still recommend it — because the foundation is that strong. Even unpatched, even without support, it outclasses most of what the RTS genre has offered since. If you can accept the balance warts as part of the experience and play around them, you'll find one of the deepest strategy games available.
Just go in knowing what you're getting. No patches are coming. No balance fixes. Relic is looking the other way.
The game deserved a better ending than this.

Amazing game. I love it. Great atmosphere, tone, art style, and gameplay.

More of the same good ol' Company of Heroes strategy fun, with a handful of small changes when compared to the first. Some key differences:

- Most captured points are no longer for a specific dedicated resource type. Instead, your Engineer unit (and some infantry) can 'reinforce' many captured points to focus them on either munitions or fuel. This allows you to tailor your captured points to your own build's needs. 

- The skill/tech tree has been revamped. Instead of picking between three branches depending on your faction, you now pre-select up to three 'specialists' that can be chosen from in-game, with each specialist having their own unique unit and support abilities. This adds an extra layer of depth and customization without being too overwhelming. 

- Infantry units die much easier when compared to CoH, and even anti-tank infantry can get destroyed by a tank if not supported with proper AT. In the original CoH, a single AT infantry unit could wreck a tank on it's own. While this change is more 'realistic', it does trivialize the use of standard infantry later game when playing skirmish matchups. 

- New unit management UI tool for easier control. Instead of a small shield that has to be hovered over which can easily be overlooked, each unit is now clearly listed on your screen for quick selection.  

If there was one complaint I have, it's the introduction of microtransactions. The first CoH did not have any special unlocked items or microtransactions of any kind. With the change to Specialists and other 'enhancement' unlocks or skins for the multiplayer skirmish, they've added a microtransaction system to 'help' you unlock things faster. While not essential for playing the game, it does feel a bit 'pay to win' when compared to the original.
Coh2 is a great squad based game although the tanks hits still confuse me a little bit.

A brutally unforgiving RTS masterpiece wrapped in a frustratingly greedy monetization scheme.

Company of Heroes 2 remains one of the most intense, strategically demanding, and deeply rewarding real-time strategy games ever created. Unlike traditional base-building games where you can just mass-produce a giant blob of tanks and right-click the enemy base, this game demands absolute, unrelenting tactical focus. The combat is incredibly lethal and dynamic; a single misplaced squad of infantry can be entirely wiped out in a split second by a well-placed artillery shell or a flanking machine gun. It forces you to constantly utilize cover, manage sightlines, and even battle the harsh Russian winter itself. When you successfully execute a coordinated flanking maneuver and watch an enemy heavy tank brew up in flames, the adrenaline rush is unmatched.

However, to experience the full breadth of this incredible tactical sandbox, you have to navigate a monetization system that feels incredibly dated and greedy. While the base game is solid, a massive amount of the game's actual strategic variety—including entire factions, specific commanders, and unique tactical abilities—is locked behind a wall of DLC and microtransactions. It can be deeply frustrating for a new player to load into a multiplayer match and get completely stomped by a highly specific, premium commander ability that they don't have access to unless they open their wallet. It is a brilliant game buried under a very "pay-for-options" business model.

Despite the heavy monetization and its age, the fact that thousands of players are still actively grinding the multiplayer ladders today speaks volumes about the core gameplay loop. For many veteran players, the gritty, heavy, and polished feel of CoH 2 still completely outshines the newer Company of Heroes 3. The steep learning curve will absolutely punish you, and you will likely lose your first dozen matches in spectacular fashion. But if you have the patience to learn the intricacies of its brutal, chaotic combat, it is an RTS experience that you will easily sink thousands of hours into.

Okay, that's your situation. You have three infantry platoons in the harsh winter, the mud is knee-deep, your men have been eating melted snow and damp cigarettes for two days, the half-track's engine sounds like an asthmatic old man about to give up, and every tree in the forest seems to be hiding a German with anger issues.

After 40 minutes of fighting, during which you moved each unit like a surgeon performing open-heart surgery, you finally managed to push the enemy back to their last checkpoint. Your engineers are desperately repairing a Sherman that has already survived more explosions than your team has any sense of reason. The air smells of gasoline, gunpowder, and terrible decisions.

Then it happens.

An enemy mortar, lost in the fog of war, probably fired by Hans, a myopic 17-year-old recruit with frozen hands, launches a shell that ricochets off a rock, lands EXACTLY on your tank under repair, kills the engineers, sets the engine ablaze, sends the infantry into a panic, and triggers a chain reaction so absurd it seems written by a drunken screenwriter.

Your Sherman explodes.
Your men fall back. A platoon accidentally steps on a mine forgotten since 1943. The enemy captures the point.

You lose the game.

Silence.

You stare at the screen like a traumatized veteran watching the last train home pull away. Your friends on Discord are screaming. You say nothing. You only hear the rain and the distant sound of your dignity leaving your body.

You close the game.

You reopen it four minutes later.
